coredata.dc:title: Isobaric vapor-liquid equilibrium of fluorobenzene with alkyl carbonates and alcohols
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Wolke, Martin
Jasch, Katharina
Scholl, Stephan
coredata.dc:description: Isobaric vapor-liquid equilibrium (VLE) data was determined experimentally for binary mixtures of fluorobenzene (FB) with methanol (MeOH), ethanol (EtOH), dimethyl carbonate (DMC), ethyl methyl carbonate (EMC), and diethyl carbonate (DEC). The measurements were conducted at total pressures of 500 mbar for alcohol mixtures and 100 mbar for alkyl carbonate mixtures. The data show azeotrope formation in the alcohol and DMC systems, while EMC and DEC mixtures exhibit nearly ideal behavior. Thermodynamic consistency was evaluated using Herington, Wisniak and Fredenslund tests, with varying outcomes. The VLE data were successfully correlated using the Non-Random Two-Liquid (NRTL) model, demonstrating good agreement with experimental results. The findings contribute to a comprehensive understanding of the separation behavior of mixtures composed of these components, which is relevant for the purification of recovered solvent mixtures during lithium-ion battery recycling.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Spent battery electrolyte
Destillation
Solvent recycling
Vapor-liquid equilibrium
Fluorobenzene
Alcohols
Alkyl carbonates

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: T–x,y diagram (left) and γ–x diagram (right) for the MeOH (1) + FB (2) system at 500 mbar. Experimental VLE data (symbols) are shown together with the correlated NRTL model (black lines).
T–x,y diagram (left) and γ–x diagram (right) for the EtOH (1) + FB (2) system at 500 mbar. Experimental VLE data (symbols) are shown together with the correlated NRTL model (black lines).

T–x,y diagram (left) and γ–x diagram (right) for the FB (1) + DMC (2) system at 100 mbar. Experimental VLE data (symbols) are shown together with the correlated NRTL model (black lines) and ideal behavior (red line).

T–x,y diagram (left) and γ–x diagram (right) for the FB (1) + EMC (2) system at 100 mbar. Experimental VLE data (symbols) are shown together with the correlated NRTL model (black lines) and ideal behavior (red line).

T–x,y diagram (left) and γ–x diagram (right) for the FB (1) + DEC (2) system at 100 mbar. Experimental VLE data (symbols) are shown together with the correlated NRTL model (black lines) and ideal behavior (red line).
Overview of VLE data available in the literature, together with the respective conditions.
Parameters and equations for calculating vapor pressure p 0 in Pa of each component.

Results of the thermodynamic consistency tests.
Parameters Δg 12, Δg 12 and α 12 for the NRTL model and root-mean-square deviation between experimental and calculated vapor phase mole fraction δ y and temperature δ T .

head.ce:title: Isobaric vapor-liquid equilibrium of fluorobenzene with alkyl carbonates and alcohols

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Chemical and Thermal Process Engineering, Technische Universität Braunschweig, Langer Kamp 7, 38106 Braunschweig, Germany

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute for Chemical and Thermal Process Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute for Chemical and Thermal Process Engineering (ICTV), Technische Universität Braunschweig,Langer Kamp 7 38106 Braunschweig, Germany

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Isobaric vapor-liquid equilibrium (VLE) data was determined experimentally for binary mixtures of fluorobenzene (FB) with methanol (MeOH), ethanol (EtOH), dimethyl carbonate (DMC), ethyl methyl carbonate (EMC), and diethyl carbonate (DEC). The measurements were conducted at total pressures of 500 mbar for alcohol mixtures and 100 mbar for alkyl carbonate mixtures. The data show azeotrope formation in the alcohol and DMC systems, while EMC and DEC mixtures exhibit nearly ideal behavior. Thermodynamic consistency was evaluated using Herington, Wisniak and Fredenslund tests, with varying outcomes. The VLE data were successfully correlated using the Non-Random Two-Liquid (NRTL) model, demonstrating good agreement with experimental results. The findings contribute to a comprehensive understanding of the separation behavior of mixtures composed of these components, which is relevant for the purification of recovered solvent mixtures during lithium-ion battery recycling.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:def-description.ce:para: regression parameters for calculating physical properties [–]
NRTL energies of interaction between the molecules 1 and 2 [J·mol−1]
enthalpy of vaporization of the pure substance at system pressure [J·mol−1]
refractive index of the pure substance [–]
deviation of refractive index from ideality [–]
vapor pressure of the pure substance [Pa]
entropy of vaporization of the pure substance at system pressure [J·mol−1·K−1]
maximum boiling temperature of the binary mixture [K]
upper limit for valid use of regression parameters [K]
minimum boiling temperature of the binary mixture [K]
lower limit for valid use of regression parameters [K]
boiling temperature of the pure substance at system pressure [K]
mole fraction in liquid phase for the component i [–]
mole fraction in vapor phase for the component i [–]
difference of compressibility factors between vapor and liquid phase [–]
NRTL non-randomness parameter for binary interaction [–]
activity coefficient for the component i [–]
root-mean-square deviation between experimental and calculated property [–]
fugacity coefficient for the component i [–]

ce:section.ce:para: Electromobility is expanding, leading to an increased production of high-voltage lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) [1], which find utilization in battery electric vehicles (BEVs). This increase, in turn, results in a corresponding rise in the number of LIBs requiring recycling [2], thereby necessitating a greater focus on the recycling of the materials employed. This focus extends beyond the solid components to the liquid electrolyte, which consists of a conductive salt, such as lithium hexafluorophosphate (LiPF6), dissolved in a solvent mixture. The electrolytes from LIBs are a mixture of substances consisting of at least two solvents, usually a linear and a cyclic carbonate, which dissolve the conducting salt [3,4]. At present, the most commonly used solvents are dimethyl carbonate (DMC), ethyl methyl carbonate (EMC), diethyl carbonate (DEC) or ethylene carbonate (EC) [3,5–7], although other solvents such as fluorobenzene (FB) can also be used [8–12]. Additives are frequently employed to the electrolyte to improve the properties and safety of LIBs, such as vinylene carbonate (VC) for enhancing lifespan and performance [6,13,14] or cyclohexylbenzene (CHB) for overcharge protection [15].

LIBs reaching the end of their lifespan or experiencing failure during the manufacturing process can be recycled through various methods [16]. One such recycling method involves the shredding of discharged LIBs, followed by a vacuum-drying of the shredded material in an inter gas atmosphere, resulting in the separation of volatile organic components from the remaining battery materials [17–21]. These components are then recovered through condensation, yielding a condensate comprising the solvents utilized and decomposition products such as methanol (MeOH) or ethanol (EtOH) [10,11,22,23], which are formed during the utilization of the LIB or during the recycling. However, components with a low vapor pressure, such as EC or CHB, and components that degrade during the use of the LIBs, such as VC, are not or only to a limited extent recovered by this process [11,24]. Based on calculations forecasting the amount of end-of-life LIBs to be recycled from BEVs batteries in Europe [2], and assuming that 6–12 % of volatile solvents [16,25] can be recovered, the material flow of recovered solvents can be estimated to reach around 63–126 kt per year for Europe in 2040. Due to the stringent purity requirements for the solvents, direct reuse of the condensate for new LIBs is not possible but requires additional purification steps.

The distillation of this multi-component mixture, which can vary significantly in the composition depending on the recycled LIBs, has been identified as a promising approach for processing the condensate to specification [11,26]. Conceptual process design of a separation and purification scheme to obtain recycled electrolyte solvent components builds on a comprehensive qualitative modeling of vapor-liquid equilibria (VLE) of all relevant mixtures at separation conditions. Frequent condensate constituents are DMC, EMC, DEC, and FB, in addition to the impurities MeOH and EtOH [10,11]. The combination of the aforementioned alkyl carbonates with each other, as well as with the alcohols, also occurs in the synthesis of these alkyl carbonates [27–40]. Consequently, these binary combinations have been published at least once in literature. Table 1 provides an overview of the openly documented VLE data.

However, no VLE data between FB and the alkyl carbonates or FB and alcohols could be found in the literature. Since these data are essential for the conceptual design of a distillative separation and purification process, the VLE data have been experimentally determined at isobaric conditions for the binary mixtures MeOH (1) + FB (2) and EtOH (1) + FB (2) at 500 mbar and FB (1) + DMC (2), FB (1) + EMC (2) and FB (1) + DEC (2) at 100 mbar. In the present work, the data have been analyzed for thermodynamic consistency and were modelled with the Non-Random Two-Liquid (NRTL) model.

Table 2 provides an overview of the employed chemicals, including their specifications and suppliers. The chemicals exhibited a purity of at least ≥ 99.90 % by mass as determined by gas chromatography (GC) and a water content of <1000 ppm, measured using Karl Fischer titration (KF). For VLE and refractive index measurements (Section 2.3 and 2.4), a series of eleven standard mixtures for the binary mixtures MeOH (1) + FB (2), EtOH (1) + FB (2), FB (1) + DMC (2), FB (1) + EMC (2) and FB (1) + DEC (2) with a mole fraction of x 1 ranging from 0.05 to 0.95 were prepared gravimetrically using a PRACTUM 224–1S balance (Sartorius Lab Instruments, Göttingen, Germany) with a resolution of ± 0.0001 g.

In order to calculate the thermodynamic consistency (Section 2.3) and to determine the NRTL parameters (Section 2.5) based on the experimental VLE data, necessary physical properties of the pure substances, such as vapor pressure p 0, calculated using Eq. (1) with parameters from Table 3 , and enthalpy of vaporization ΔH vap, calculated using Eq. (2) with parameters from Table 4 , were to consider.

The parameters and the critical temperature T c, provided in Table 5 , for calculating these physical properties for MeOH, EtOH, DMC and DEC were obtained from the software Simulis Thermodynamic, Version 2.0.46 (Fives ProSim, Labège, France), which utilizes the pure component database DIPPR 801 from the Design Institute for Physical Properties (DIPPR). The vapor pressure of EMC was derived from [47], while the enthalpy of vaporization was calculated using the Clapeyron equation Eq. (3) [48].

Isobaric VLE measurements of the binary systems MeOH (1) + FB (2) and EtOH (1) + FB (2) were conducted at a total pressure p = 500 mbar, while FB (1) + DMC (2), FB (1) + EMC (2) and FB (1) + DEC (2) were measured at p = 100 mbar utilizing the dynamic still, VLE 100 (PILODIST GmbH, Meckenheim, Germany). A comprehensive description of the experimental setup can be found in [49]. The substance mixture was heated in the mixing chamber and rising vapor entrained liquid to the Cottrell pump, where turbulent flow enabled a rapid establishment of phase equilibrium and increases mass transfer. Subsequent to the passing of the Cottrell pump, the phases underwent separation due to density differences. The vapor phase was condensed, and both phases were returned to the mixing chamber to ensure continuous circulation. The temperature T was measured with a calibrated Pt-100 sensor (resolution: ± 0.01 K) with a combined standard uncertainty calculated to u c(T) = 0.05 K. The total pressure p was controlled through a pneumatic and electrical control regulated by the software VLE 100D and measured with a pressure probe (resolution ± 0.03 mbar) with a combined standard uncertainty calculated to u c(p) = 1.17 mbar. Once a constant temperature and pressure had been achieved for a minimum of 15 min, the system was considered to be stationary. Samples of the liquid and vapor condensate (approximately 2 ml each) were taken concurrently and analyzed by measuring the refractive index to determine the liquid and vapor phase mole fraction x 1 and y 1, see Section 2.4. The activity coefficients γ 1 and γ 2 were calculated from the experimental data according to Eq. (4).
(4)
γ i
=
y i
· p ·
φ i
x i
·
p
i
0

Fugacity coefficients of a component in the mixture φ i were determined using the Soave-Redlich-Kwong equation of state [50], based on the pure component data provided in Table 5. The Poynting correction was neglected. Resulting fugacity coefficients range from 0.981 to 0.997. Table 5 further includes measured boiling temperatures and vapor pressures at defined pressures, along with literature data and values calculated via Eq. (1) using parameters from Table 3.

Thermodynamic consistency was verified using the area test from Herington [56–58] and the two point-to-point tests proposed by Wisniak [58–60] and Fredenslund et al. [61]. The Herington consistency test involved calculating the areas of the natural logarithm of the ratio of the activity coefficients ln(γ 1/γ 2) in dependence of x 1 below and above the abscissa. These were calculated segmentally between the measuring points using the trapeze rule. The dimensionless parameters D and J were determined using Eqs. (5) and (6), with T max and T min denoting the maximum and minimum boiling temperature of the binary systems, respectively. The criterion for consistency is defined as |D − J| < 10.
(5)
D = 100 ·

The thermodynamic consistency test, as defined by Wisniak [59], is based on Eqs. (7) to (10). This method takes into account the enthalpy of vaporization

Thermodynamic consistency is considered to be fulfilled if D, calculated via Eq. (11), satisfies D < 3. For systems where the enthalpy of vaporization has been estimated, consistency is assumed if D < 5.

In contrast, the thermodynamic consistency test proposed by Fredenslund et al. [61] is described by Eqs. (12) to (15). In this approach, experimental values of G E are first calculated using Eq. (7). These values are then modelled using a series expansion in terms of Legendre polynomials L k, as shown in Eq. (13). The calculated γ 1 are then determined via Eq. (14) and used to calculate predicted values of y 1 by rearranging Eq. (4), and the total pressure p via Eq. (15). The polynomial coefficients a k are fitted by minimizing the squared deviation between the experimental and calculated total pressures. Five polynomial coefficients a k have been used for this fitting procedure [60–62].

≤ 0.01

The composition of binary mixtures was determined by measuring the refractive index of the mixture n D using Abbemat 500 refractometer (Anton Paar, Ostfildern-Scharnhausen, Germany, accuracy: ± 2 × 10−5) at a temperature of T = 293.15 K and a wavelength of λ = 589 nm. For this purpose, the refractive index of the eleven standard mixtures mentioned in Section 2.1 and of the pure substances was determined in advance by triple measurement. From the measured refractive indices, the deviation of refractive index from ideality Δn D was calculated by rearranging Eq. (17) to give Eq. (18). The deviations of refractive index from ideality were then fitted to the Redlich & Kister approach, Eq. (19), with N = 2. The measured data for the refractive indices and the deviation values are given in the supplementary data, Table A1 and Fig. A1.

i

Samples from the VLE measurements (Section 2.3) were analysed in the same manner as previously described, with the molar fraction then being determined by means of numerical calculation using Eqs. (17) to (19), in combination with the Monte Carlo method, with respect to the determined parameters, listed in Table 6 , and the covariances matrix. A total of 500,000 Monte Carlo simulations were conducted. This calculation procedure was also used to calculate the uncertainties of the mole fractions simultaneously.

Experimentally measured VLE data were modelled with the NRTL model [63], Eqs. (20) to (23), while taking into account non-ideal vapor phase behavior via fugacity coefficients obtained from the Soave-Redlich-Kwong equation of state [50], to determine the interaction energy parameters Δg 12, Δg 21 and the non-randomness parameter α 12. In line with recent findings [64] α 12 was treated as an adjustable parameter in the range of 0 to 1. The activity coefficients γ 1 and γ 2 used for the parameter regression were calculated from the experimental data using Eq. (4).

The model parameters were determined by minimizing the objective function OF in Eq. (24) except for FB (1) + EMC (2) and FB (1) + DEC (2), for which the OF in Eq. (25) was used. In all cases, the optimization was performed with respect to the number of datapoints NDP and the number of components NC [65].

The experimental VLE data for binary mixtures between FB and alcohols, see Table 7 and Table 8
, reveals non-ideal behaviour, characterized by a positive deviation from Raoult's law (γ i > 1), as illustrated in Figs. 1 and 2
. It is evident from the experimental data that both mixtures form a low-boiling azeotrope, with the azeotropic point at x 1 = 0.639 for MeOH (1) + FB (2) and x 1 = 0.445 for EtOH (1) + FB (2).

This behavior can be explained by the lower polarity of the halogenated hydrocarbon FB, which is distinct from the alcohols that possess a polar part with the -OH group and a non-polar part with the alkyl chain. This leads to positive deviations from Raoult's law, as the attractive interactions of unlike molecules are weakened compared to the like molecules [66]. This explanation is further substantiated by the activity coefficients, which are smaller for EtOH (1) + FB (2) than for MeOH (1) + FB (2) over the entire range of the measured data. This can be attributed to the longer non-polar alkyl chain in EtOH and the associated higher non-polar proportion.

The binary mixtures of FB and alkyl carbonates, listed in Table 9 , Table 10 and Table 11
, exhibit almost ideal behavior, except for FB (1) + DMC (2), as the length of the alkyl chain increases. To facilitate comparison, the experimental data and the ideal VLE data, calculated using Eqs. (26) and (27), are displayed in Figs. 3 , 4 and 5
.
(26)
x 1
=
p ·
p 1 0
p 1 0
−
p 2 0
(27)
y 1
=
x 1
·
p 1 0
p

The systems FB (1) + EMC (2) and FB (1) + DEC (2) exhibit nearly ideal behavior, as evidenced by the close agreement between experimental and ideal VLE data, as well as the activity coefficients approaching unity. The activity coefficients range from γ 1 = 1.008 – 1.025 and γ 2 = 0.824 – 1.008 for FB (1) + EMC (2), and from γ 1 = 0.892 – 1.023 and γ 2 = 0.596 – 0.965 for FB (1) + DEC (2). It should be noted that although the activity coefficients γ 2 continuously decrease with increasing mole fraction x 1, thereby deviating further from the ideal value, the associated uncertainty in γ 2, calculated via error propagation, also increases significantly. Given the low values of γ 2 in both mixtures, this results in a non-negligible error, as shown in Figs. 3, 4 and 5.

In contrast, the mixture FB (1) + DMC (2) demonstrates a positive deviation from Raoult's law (γᵢ > 1) and forms a low-boiling azeotrope with the azeotropic point at x 1 = 0.965 and T = 297.53 K as predicted by NRTL (see Section 3.2). This behaviour, analogous to that observed in alcohols, can be ascribed to the functional groups of the carbonates. While the carbonate group (–O–C(=O)–O–) generally results in low polarity for alkyl carbonates, the attached alkyl chains contribute additional non-polar characteristics. However, since DMC has a higher polarity than EMC and DEC, its intermolecular interactions with the non-polar FB are weakened, leading to the observed positive deviation.

As can be seen from the γ–x diagrams in Figs. 1–5, the activity coefficients γ i as a function of the molar fraction x 1 show no anomalous tendencies and tend to approach unity with increasing molar fraction x i. This can be considered as a basic test of consistency [58]. The results of the thermodynamic consistency tests described in Section 2.3 are summarized in Table 12 .

It is evident that none of the binary systems fulfil all three of the established test criteria. However, it is noteworthy that each system successfully passes at least one of the tests applied. Of the five systems, MeOH (1) + FB (2) and EtOH (1) + FB (2) were found to pass both the Herington and Wisniak tests. However, both mixtures failed to the Fredenslund criterion. In contrast, the FB (1) + DMC (2) system passed the Fredenslund test, but failed the Herington and Wisniak tests. The FB (1) + EMC (2) system passed the Wisniak test but failed the other two, while the FB (1) + DEC (2) system passed both the Wisniak and Fredenslund tests, failing only the Herington test. A number of factors may be involved in the failure of certain tests. In particular, the increasing uncertainties of γ i, with decreasing x i also have an impact on the thermodynamic consistency results, and may provide an explanation for some of the observed failures. This is particularly the case for the mixtures of FB and alkyl carbonates, where the activity coefficients are close to one, leading to an increased sensitivity to uncertainties [58]. It is also important to note that neglecting the heat of mixing in the consistency test may also have an effect on the assessment of the thermodynamic consistency. The different results of the tests are not unexpected as the limitations of existing consistency tests are known and recognized [60]. However, for the present work, the results provide valuable initial information for further investigations into the purification of such material systems.

The NRTL model was employed to fit the experimental data, resulting in the parameters presented in Table 13 . These parameters were utilized to calculate the modelled VLE data, as depicted in Figs. 1–5. It can be observed that the model adequately reproduces the experimental data. However, slight deviations between calculated and experimental activity coefficients γ i for the alkyl carbonate systems can be observed for decreasing molar fractions x i, which can also be attributed to the influence of the error propagation mentioned in Section 3.1. Furthermore, it should be noted that the azeotropic point of FB (1) + DMC (2) could not be adequately reflected. According to NRTL, this point is determined at x1 = 0.965 and T = 297.53 K. However, experimental data suggests that the azeotropic point must be at x1 > 0.958 and T < 297.33 K. While the molar fraction may be accurately determined, it should be noted that deviations in temperature, as evidenced by the experimental data, are likely to occur. The deviation can be explained by the fact that not enough measurement data are available in the area around the azeotropic point. Therefore, it is recommended that measurement data be determined in this area and possibly for additional pressures in order to improve the modelling of FB (1) + DMC (2).

The experimental VLE data provide valuable insights into the phase behavior of fluorobenzene with alcohols and alkyl carbonates under isobaric vacuum conditions. The results indicate that alcohol mixtures exhibit non-ideal behavior with positive deviations from Raoult's law, resulting in a low-boiling azeotrope with the azeotropic point at x 1 = 0.639 for MeOH (1) + FB (2) and x 1 = 0.445 for EtOH (1) + FB (2), whereas carbonate mixtures approach close to ideality, with the exception of FB (1) + DMC (2), which also forms a low-boiling azeotrope.

None of the binary systems investigated fully satisfies all three thermodynamic consistency tests applied, although each system passes at least one of them. MeOH (1) + FB (2) and EtOH (1) + FB (2) pass both the Herington and Wisniak tests but fail the Fredenslund criterion. In contrast, FB (1) + DMC (2) meets only the Fredenslund test, FB (1) + EMC (2) passes only the Wisniak test, and FB (1) + DEC (2) satisfies both the Wisniak and Fredenslund tests. Deviations may be due to increasing uncertainties in the activity coefficients at higher mole fractions, especially in systems containing FB and alkyl carbonates. Additionally, neglecting the heat of mixing may affect consistency evaluations.

The determined NRTL parameters accurately describe the VLE data in general. Notable deviations between experimental and calculated values were observed for the FB (1) + DMC (2) system near the azeotropic point, which may be related to an insufficient number of experimental data points in this region. Additional measurements near the azeotropic composition or under different conditions could refine predictions for the FB (1) + DMC (2) system.

These findings are of particular relevance to the distillative purification of recovered solvents, which are initially used as electrolyte solvents in LIBs, and can be obtained during LIB recycling, facilitating the conceptual design of efficient solvent recovery and purification processes. Future studies will focus on extending the dataset to include additional pressures and compositions to improve process design and optimization.
This research was funded by Federal Ministry for Economic Affairs and Climate Action, grant number 16BZF342F.

Martin Wolke: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Software, Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Katharina Jasch: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Stephan Scholl: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We would like to extend our gratitude to all the students who have contributed to this work through their endeavours. Their commitment and assistance have proved to be invaluable.

Equilib. 321 (2012) 25–43. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.fluid.2012.02.010.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Tests for the consistency of experimental isobaric vapour−liquid equilibrium data

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E. Herington, Tests for the consistency of experimental isobaric vapour−liquid equilibrium data, J. Inst. Pet. 37 (1951) 457–470.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The Herington test for thermodynamic consistency

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Wisniak, The Herington test for thermodynamic consistency, Ind. Eng. Chem. Res. 33 (1994) 177–180. https://doi.org/10.1021/ie00025a025.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An assessment of thermodynamic consistency tests for vapor-liquid equilibrium data

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Wisniak, A. Apelblat, H. Segura, An assessment of thermodynamic consistency tests for vapor-liquid equilibrium data, Phys. Chem. Liq. 35 (1997) 1–58. https://doi.org/10.1080/00319109708030571.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A new test for the thermodynamic consistency of vapor-liquid equilibrium

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Wisniak, A new test for the thermodynamic consistency of vapor-liquid equilibrium, Ind. Eng. Chem. Res. 32 (1993) 1531–1533. https://doi.org/10.1021/ie00019a030.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A fresh look at the thermodynamic consistency of vapour-liquid equilibria data

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Wisniak, J. Ortega, L. Fernández, A fresh look at the thermodynamic consistency of vapour-liquid equilibria data, J. Chem. Thermodyn. 105 (2017) 385–395. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jct.2016.10.038.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Vapor-liquid Equilibria Using UNIFAC: a Group Contribution Method

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Fredenslund, J. Gmehling, P.
